coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots using Daucus carota subsp. sativus roots for mitomycin drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: D’souza, Stephanie L.
Chettiar, Shiva Shankaran
Koduru, Janardhan Reddy
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report one-step eco-friendly method for the fabrication of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using Daucus carota subsp. sativus (carrot) roots as a carbon source. The as-fabricated CDs were characterized by spectroscopic and transmission electron microscopic techniques. The as-synthesized CDs acted as nano-vehicles for delivery of mitomycin drug. The as-synthesized CDs were effectively interacted with mitomycin drug via hydrogen bonding, leading to break hydrogen bonding at mildly acidic tumor extracellular microenvironment (pH ∼6.80) and to release of mitomycin. Importantly, mitomycin drug loaded CDs exhibits high affinity towards cancer cell membrane because of their biocompatible and ultra-small size, which results the internalization of mitomycin-CDs by Bacillus subtilis cells with high degree. The in vitro experimental results revealed that mitomycin loaded CDs nanocarriers were effectively entered into the cells, suggesting that mitomycin-loaded CDs exhibited pH dependent mitomycin release behavior. Furthermore, the fluorescent CDs exhibited biocompatible nature towards MCF-7 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent CDs
Mitomycin
Bacillus subtilis
MCF-7 cells
UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopic techniques

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of mitomycin-loaded CDs and releasing of mitomycin from mitomycin-loaded CDs
Cytotoxicity and cells imaging ability of CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Science and Technology, Government of India, India for Inspire
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Kerala
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is dedicated to Ms. Stephanie L. D’souza, who was listed as a First co-author, DST- Inspire Ph.D student in the Department of Applied Chemistry, SVNIT from 2012 to 2015, passed away on 3rd May 2015 in Ahmedabad, due to heart stroke. This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India, India for Inspire Ph.D programme. SKK greatly acknowledges the Director, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat for the financial support to this work (Dean (R&C)/1503/2013-2014). This research was partially supported by Kwangwoon University Research Grant 2017. The authors thank Dr. Karuna Rawat for her help during data collection. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the fabrication of fluorescent CDs via hydrothermal method using carrot as a carbon source.

(a) UV–visible, excitation and emission spectra of CDs derived from carrot as a carbon source (Inset: CDs under daylight and UV light). (b) Study of excitation-dependent behavior of CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 520 nm with a progressively increasing 20 nm.
HR-TEM image of CDs derived from carrot as carbon source at (a) 20 nm and (b) 100 nm scale bars. (c) DLS of CDs.

Fluorescence microscopic images of Bacillus subtilis cells incubated with CDs and bright field fluorescence images were taken at excitations (a) 330–385 nm, emission 420 nm (blue) and (b) excitation 510–550 nm, emission 590 nm (red). (c) Fluorescence microscopic image of Bacillus subtilis cells without CDs and (d) merged image of Bacillus subtilis cells with CDs in bright field. Images were taken at 10 μm scale bar. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This work was orally presented at PITTCON-2017, 5th–9th March, 2017, Chicago, USA.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots using Daucus carota subsp. sativus roots for mitomycin drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat, 395007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: S. V. National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Shree Ramkrishna Institute of Computer Education and Applied Sciences, Surat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shree Ramkrishna Institute of Computer Education and Applied Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, Kwangwoon University, Nowon-gu, Seoul, 139-701, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report one-step eco-friendly method for the fabrication of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using Daucus carota subsp. sativus (carrot) roots as a carbon source. The as-fabricated CDs were characterized by spectroscopic and transmission electron microscopic techniques. The as-synthesized CDs acted as nano-vehicles for delivery of mitomycin drug. The as-synthesized CDs were effectively interacted with mitomycin drug via hydrogen bonding, leading to break hydrogen bonding at mildly acidic tumor extracellular microenvironment (pH ∼6.80) and to release of mitomycin. Importantly, mitomycin drug loaded CDs exhibits high affinity towards cancer cell membrane because of their biocompatible and ultra-small size, which results the internalization of mitomycin-CDs by Bacillus subtilis cells with high degree. The in vitro experimental results revealed that mitomycin loaded CDs nanocarriers were effectively entered into the cells, suggesting that mitomycin-loaded CDs exhibited pH dependent mitomycin release behavior. Furthermore, the fluorescent CDs exhibited biocompatible nature towards MCF-7 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, carbon dots (CDs) have proven to be promising rising stars in many research areas including sensing, bioimaging, biomedicine and drug delivery [1–3]. Since the CDs were obtained by facile synthetic approaches using natural resources as raw materials [4–8] and exhibited unique optical and physicochemical properties than the semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes [9–11]. In order to improve their physico-chemical properties, carbon dots have been synthesized by using various materials resources as carbon sources including banana juice [12], orange waste [13] and watermelon [14] peels, corn flour [15], histidine [16], orange juice [17], soy milk [18], kitchen waste [19], green tea [20], pomelo peel [21], coffee [22], egg white [23], egg yolk oil [24], and tobacco [25], respectively. Using these approaches, carbon dots were successfully synthesized with good quantum yield and applied them as probes for sensing and bioimaging. Importantly, all of these methods were successfully fabricated CDs with multifunctional groups, showing non-toxicity and good biocompatibility towards various cell lines.

Carbon dots have also been recognized as biocompatible nanocarriers for various drug delivery systems [26–27]. Several research groups have been used CDs as drug carriers for delivery of various drugs and studied their biocompatible nature with various cells. For example, various papers have been illustrated that the use of CDs for delivery of doxorubicin in various cancer cells [28–35]. Furthermore, CDs were conjugated with chlorine e6 - hyaluronate (CDs-Ce6-HA) and used as intracellular delivery systems to B16F10 melanoma cells in tumor model mice [36]. Wang’s group loaded the cationic anticancer drug doxorubicin onto the CDs via electrostatic interactions and studied their drug releasing kinetics in HeLa cells [37]. Gong et al. fabricated P- and N- atoms-doped CDs for anticancer drug (doxorubicin) delivery and biological imaging [38]. Sachdev et al. fabricated CDs-based hydrogels with an anticancer drug (5-fluorouracil) for the qualitative and quantitative assessment of cellular uptake [39]. Apart from these, carbon dots have also been used as nanocarriers for various drugs delivery including lisinopril [40], flutamide [41], boldine [42], curcumin [43], vancomycin [44] and cisplatin (IV) [45], respectively. These studies revealed that the CDs have proven to be promising drug carrying vehicles for slow and sustained release of drugs, allowing to maintain an effective drug concentration in the tumor area and to increase the cell uptake care, which improves the utilization of the drug with high degree of drug administration.

In this work, we synthesized fluorescent CDs using Daucus carota subsp. sativus (carrot) roots as a carbon source through hydrothermal method. The synthesized fluorescent CDs are well dispersed in water with an average size of 2.30 ± 1.0 nm, and existing with different functional groups, which allow them to act as vehicles for drug loading and sustained release. The synthesized CDs were loaded with mitomycin drug. For mytomycin loading, the CDs and mitomycin were vortexed in PBS pH at 7.4 and mitomycin drug loading and releasing were evaluated by UV–visible and FT-IR techniques. Biocompatible nature of CDs and mitomycin-loaded CDs was confirmed by MTT assay, suggesting that the CDs and mitomycin-loaded CDs can also be used as bio-imaging probes in cancer cells and Bacillus subtilis cells. Thus, unique optical (fluorescence) and physico-chemical properties (morphology, size, and zeta potential) of CDs allow them to behave as a trigger at lower intracellular pH to release mitomycin from the CDs surfaces with high degree.
Mitomycin was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Dialysis membrane-70 was obtained from HiMedia Laboratories Pvt., India.

The fluorescent CDs was typically fabricated using Daucus carota subsp. sativus (carrot) roots as a carbon source through hydrothermal method as follows. Briefly, the fresh carrot roots were washed thoroughly with deionized water to remove impurities from the roots. Then, 250 g of carrot was cut into small pieces and transferred to a 750-mL stainless steel autoclave vessel. The autoclave reactor is heated at 170 °C for 12 h, resulting to change the color of the solution from red to brown. The reaction vessel was cooled and the resulted solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm. The obtained solution was dialyzed in a membrane-70 tubing (molecular weight cutoff ∼1000 Da) against distilled water for 48 h. The brown CDs solution was kept at 4 °C until needed for further experimentation.

For mitomycin-loaded CDs, 1 mL of CDs solution (6 mg mL−1) and 1 mL of mitomycin (300 μg mL−1) were mixed with PBS at pH 7.4 and then stirred for 30 min. The above solution was vortexed for 3 h. The solution was dialyzed using a membrane-70 tubing (molecular weight cutoff ∼1000 Da) against deionized for 1 h to remove unreacted mitomycin or CDs. Mitomycin-loaded CDs solution was stored at 4 °C in the dark, until needed for further experiments. The CDs-drug releasing efficiency was studied at physiological condition. Briefly, 20 mL of mitomycin-loaded CDs was taken into a fresh dialysis membrane bag and then dialyzed against 80 mL of PBS pH at 7.4 under intermittent stirring at 37 ºC. The kinetics of mitomycin drug release were studied by UV–Vis spectrometry at different time intervals. The mitomycin drug release efficiency was evaluated by the described formula as shown below:
[(Xn − X)/Xn] × 100
Where, X is the absorbance at 0 h and Xn is the absorbance at different hour i.e. X1, X2…. n for 1 to 9 h.

The cytotoxicity of pure mitomycin, bare CDs, mitomycin-loaded CDs against MCF-7 cells was evaluated by MTT method. The MCF-7 cells (1 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h. The culture medium was treated separately with pure mitomycin, bare CDs and mitomycin-loaded CDs samples at different concentrations (10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 750 and 1000 μg/mL), and then incubated for 24 h. The above samples were incubated at 37 °C for 4.0 h by adding 50 μL of MTT solution (2 mg/mL). To quantify cell viability, the culture medium was removed and fresh medium containing pure mitomycin, bare CDs and mitomycin-loaded CDs was added to each well and then incubated for 24 h. After removing culture medium, 150 μL of DMSO was added to each cell and then incubated another 15 min. The cell viability was evaluated by measuring absorbance at 570 and 630 nm with spectrophotometry. The following formula (OD, optical density) was used to estimate the cell viability
Cell viability (%) = (A570 − A630) treated cells/(A570 − A630) controlled cells × 100

The imaging properties of bare CDs, Bacillus subtilis cells imaging studies were evaluated by confocal laser scanning microscopy at 405 nm excitation laser. Briefly, Bacillus subtilis bacteria were grown on a sterilized solid LB medium (27% peptone, 13.5% yeast extract, 27% NaCl, and 32.5% agar) for 12 h. From the above, a colony of bacterial strain was added into sterilized LB medium. The bacteria consisting LB medium was incubated at 37 °C. The UV–visible spectroscopy was used to evaluated the growth of bacteria and the bacteria cell account was estimated based on optical density at 600 nm. After bacterial growth, Bacillus subtilis bacterial cells were washed with PBS (pH 7.4) and the bacterial cells were re-suspended in the PBS (pH 7.4) solution that contains CDs (500 μg/mL). The Bacillus subtilis conjugated CDs suspensions were incubated at 37 °C for 3 h and then the mixture was centrifuged to pellet the Bacillus subtilis conjugated CDs, the supernatant was removed and the pellet was re-suspended in PBS buffer. The Bacillus subtilis conjugated CDs were fixed on slides by 4% paraformaldehyde and mounted using glycerol (50%), and then images were captured by confocal fluorescence microscopy.

The as-synthesized CDs were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (JEOL 3010, Japan), Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA), Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) (Shimadzu, FT-IR 8400S, Japan) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK), respectively. Cell images were measured using a fluorescence microscopy (Carl Zeiss 510 LSM, Jena, Germany).

In order to confirm the surface functional groups on CDs, FT-IR spectroscopy was used to confirm the organic functional groups on the CDs surfaces. As depicted in Supporting information of Fig. S1, the broad band at 3431 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of OH and NH2 groups on the surfaces of CDs. The weak peaks 2827 and 2893 cm−1 belong to CH2– and CH3 groups stretching vibrations. The peak at 1666 cm−1 was ascribed due to stretching vibration of CO group, and the peak at 1624 cm−1 corresponds to NH bending vibrations on the surfaces of CDs. The characteristic peaks in the region of 1493–1245 cm−1 belong to stretching vibrations of various organic groups (CC, CN and CCO) on the surfaces of CDs, suggesting the formation of CDs with multifunctional groups. The size and morphology of CDs were measured by HRTEM and DLS. Fig. 2 a and b shows the as-synthesized CDs are found to be ultra-small spherical size and dispersed in water. The hydrodynamic radius of as-synthesized CDs is 2.30 ± 1.0 nm (Fig. 2c). The HRTEM images show that as-synthesized CDs are well resolved lattice fringes with an average in-plane lattice spacing of ∼0.221 nm, which is very close to the (001) diffraction facets of graphite carbon [47]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs was shown in Supporting information of Fig. S2a. The XRD pattern of CDs shows the three sharp diffraction peaks centered at 28.5°, 32.4°, 40.1°, 43.2° and 50.2°, which are due to the disordered carbon atoms and crystalline nature of synthesized CDs. The fluorescence decay curve of CDs was fitted by a dual-exponential formula with an average lifetime of 6.80 ns (Supporting information of Fig. S2b), which is close to those of other reported CDs [42–47], and these results suggest that the CDs consist of multifunctional groups, which allow to make them as promising carriers for drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of mitomycin-loaded CDs and releasing of mitomycin from mitomycin-loaded CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The drug carrying ability of CDs was investigated by loading mitomycin drug and studied their releasing kinetics at physiological pH 7.4. To this, the absorption spectra of mitomycin-loaded CDs were investigated at different ratios 0:10 and 10:0 (CDs:mitomycin, v/v), and 100 μg/mL of mitomycin was used for mitomycin-loaded CDs. It can be observed that as-synthesized CDs exhibit the maximum absorbances at 330, 345 and 365 nm whereas pure mitomycin shows the absorption peak at 375 nm (Supporting information of Fig. S3). However, the absorption spectra were slightly changed with increasing volume of mitomycin. The characteristic peaks of CDs (330, 345 and 365 nm) and of mitomycin (375 nm) were slightly shifted, and appearing one broad peak 365 nm using CDs:mitomycin volumes at 6:4 (v/v), which indicates the formation of mitomycin-loaded CDs.

Furthermore, we also studied the chemical groups of pure mitomycin and mitomycin-loaded CDs by FT-IR spectroscopy (Supporting information of Fig. S4). As can be seen in the FT-IR spectrum of pure mitomycin (Supporting information of S4a), the peaks at 3381 and 1729 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH and CO groups, respectively. The sharp peak at 1064 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of O (ether) group of mitomycin. Similarly, aromatic CH stretching was observed at 3090 cm−1 and NH bending of amide group was observed at 1634 cm−1. Several peaks at 1250–1100 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of COC group of mitomycin. However, it can be noticed that the characteristic peaks of hydroxy and amino groups were slightly shifted, suggesting that the interaction of mitomycin on the surfaces of CDs. Importantly, the stretching vibration of CO of mitomycin was shifted to 1786 cm−1, and the broad peak at 3200–3400 cm−1 confirming the involvement of NH2 and OH groups of both mitomycin and CDs in hydrogen bonding, which suggests that mitomycin was effectively loaded on the surfaces of CDs via non-covalent interactions.

To examine the releasing efficiency of mitomycin drug from mitomycin-loaded CDs, we studied the mitomycin releasing kinetics from the surfaces at different time intervals from 0 to 9 h at pH 7.4 (Supporting information of Fig. S5a). These studies revealed that the percentage of mitomycin was gradually increased with increasing time, revealing that the releasing of mitomycin from the surfaces of CDs. Furthermore, the release rate was gradually increased with increasing time up to 3.0 h and then became plateau, suggesting the release of mitomycin from the surfaces of CDs at 3.0 h with high degree. Furthermore, several statistical models (Primary order, zero order, Hixon–Crowell, Higuchi, Wibull, Baker–Lonsdale, Hopenfen burg, Sequential layerl) were used for the estimation of drug release kinetics [48]. Based on these, we assume that the mitomycin-loaded CDs drug delivery strategy belong to Hixon–Crowell model, suggesting the drug releasing ability may depend on the composition of materials, size and active organic groups on the CDs surfaces, which reveals that mitomycin drug releasing may prove as high therapeutic efficacy for basal chemotherapy with fast-acting dosage.

The cytotoxicity nature of fabricated CDs was evaluated by incubating MCF-7 cells with control, bare CDs, mitomycin and mitomycin-loaded CDs and the results were shown in Supporting information of Fig. S5b. The MTT assay was used to investigate the cytotoxicity of CDs on MCF-7 cells. It can be seen that favorable viabilities of MCF-7 cells of 89.0% and 81.00% were achieved by incubating cells with bare CDs and pure mitomycin drug. However, it can be noticed that a much lower cell viability of 60.0% was achieved when incubating with mitomycin-loaded CDs system, demonstrating that the mitomycin-loaded CDs carriers exhibit significant improvement on the transportation of mitomycin into the target cells interior, which causes to kill the cells via accumulation of mitomycin drug.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and cells imaging ability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate cytotoxicity of CDs, MTT assay was performed on MCF-7 cells using different concentrations of CDs (10–1000 μg mL−1). As shown in Supporting information of Fig. S6, the percentage of cell inhibiting rate is increasing with increasing concentration of CDs and cell inhibition rate was ∼20% even at the concentration of 1000 μg mL−1. These results suggest that almost 95% of the cells were found to be active even at high concentration of CDs, confirming the low toxicity, and their excellent biocompatibility, which make them as promising carriers for the development of drug delivery system.

In order to explore the imaging ability of as-synthesized CDs, cell imaging studies were performed on Bacillus subtilis bacterial cells. Fig. 3 represents the fluorescence microscopic images of Bacillus subtilis cells under different exciter filters, and in only fluorescence mode by using CDs as fluorescent imaging probes. Fig. 3a and b shows the fluorescence images of CDs labelled Bacillus subtilis cells exhibit blue and red emissions using CDs as imaging probes at different excitation wavelengths. Similarly, we have also taken image of CDs labelled Bacillus subtilis cells under bright field with fluorescence mode (Fig. 3d), revealing that CDs show the excitation-dependent FL behavior that allow multiple color emission in the bacterial cells. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the bright field image of Bacillus subtilis cells (without CDs) did not exhibit fluorescence under different exciter filters, confirming that the internalization of CDs by bacterial cells. Since ultra-small CDs with multi-functional groups can easily internalize by cells and reach to either the cytoplasm or the cell nuclei. These results reveal that CDs derived from carrot can be used as potential candidates for cell imaging and drug delivery system.

In summary, ultra-small fluorescent CDs were derived from Daucus carota subsp. sativus (carrot) roots by single-step hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized CDs exhibited excitation-dependent behavior with quantum yield of 7.60%. The as-synthesized CDs acted as drug delivery system for mitomycin and exhibited biocompatible nature. The as-synthesized CDs are easily internalized into MCF-7 and bacterial cells and exhibited good biocompatibility in vitro. The drug release results demonstrated that CDs could effectively hold mitomycin drug and released effectively from the surfaces of CDs, suggesting the mitomycin-loaded CDs possess good drug controlled realizing system. Therefore, the CDs derived from carrot act as effective drug vehicles, and offer good specificity and selectivity for the development of mitomycin drug delivery system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is dedicated to Ms. Stephanie L. D’souza, who was listed as a First co-author, DST- Inspire Ph.D student in the Department of Applied Chemistry, SVNIT from 2012 to 2015, passed away on 3rd May 2015 in Ahmedabad, due to heart stroke. This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India, India for Inspire Ph.D programme. SKK greatly acknowledges the Director, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat for the financial support to this work (Dean (R&C)/1503/2013-2014). This research was partially supported by Kwangwoon University Research Grant 2017. The authors thank Dr. Karuna Rawat for her help during data collection.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijleo.2017.12.200

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots using watermelon peel as a carbon source
Dual functional carbon dots derived from corn flour via a simple one-pot hydro thermal route
A carbon dot based biosensor for melamine detection by fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents

Synthesis of carbon dots from kitchen waste: conversion of waste to value added product
Extremely high inhibition activity of photoluminescent carbon nanodots toward cancer cells
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Synthesis and analytical applications of photoluminescent carbon nanodots

Carbon dots—emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics
Shining carbon dots: synthesis and biomedical and optoelectronic applications
Doxorubicin conjugated functionalizable carbon dots for nucleus targeted delivery and enhanced therapeutic efficacy
Swarming carbon dots for folic acid mediated delivery of doxorubicin and biological imaging
Carbon nanodots featuring efficient FRET for real-time monitoring of drug delivery and two-photon imaging
Amphiphilic carbon dots as versatile vectors for nucleic acid and drug delivery
Doxorubicin-loaded environmentally friendly carbon dots as a novel drug delivery system for nucleus targeted cancer therapy

DNA–carbon dots function as fluorescent vehicles for drug delivery
Photodynamic therapy of melanoma skin cancer using carbon dot – chlorin e6 – hyaluronate conjugate
Highly fluorescent carbon dots for visible sensing of doxorubicin release based on efficient nanosurface energy transfer
Phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped hollow carbon dot as a nanocarrier for doxorubicin delivery and biological imaging
Carbon dots incorporated polymeric hydrogels as multifunctional platform for imaging and induction of apoptosis in lung cancer cells
Green synthetic approach for synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for lisinopril drug delivery system and their confirmations in the cells
Fluorescent carbon dots derived from vancomycin for flutamide drug delivery and cell imaging
Synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from dried shrimps for cell imaging and boldine drug delivery system
Lignin derived reduced fluorescence carbon dots with theranostic approaches: nano-drug-carrier and bioimaging
Carbon quantum dot tailored calcium alginate hydrogel for pH responsive controlled delivery of vancomycin
Dual-responsive carbon dots for tumor extracellular microenvironment triggered targeting and enhanced anticancer drug delivery
Chemical composition, functional properties and processing of carrot—a review
Drug-derived bright and color-tunable N-doped carbon dots for cell imaging and sensitive detection of Fe3+ in living cells
Controlled delivery of dopamine hydrochloride using surface modified carbon dots for neuro diseases
